hard_spots.problem: Code accesses `start_indices[i]` and `end_indices[i]` without yet explaining what these fields mean. The comment says 'efficient path' but readers haven't seen FlatLogprobs structure (defined in §10.6). Forward reference creates momentary confusion.

The loop finds 'rightmost contiguous block of fallback tokens' but the code comment is vague ('find the boundary'). Reader must reverse-engineer the loop logic (iterate backward, set clean_end=j while decoding returns U+FFFD, break when it doesn't). A one-sentence description of the intent would help.

Loop iterates `num_ctx in range(1, max_ctx+1)`. If all 4 iterations end with U+FFFD, the function returns empty string (line 474). The semantics of this return value (incomplete byte sequence, will be completed by a later token) is only stated in the recap line 491, not at the code site where it's returned.

States that first m−1 byte tokens return '', last one returns full char, so no duplication. But WHY does the algorithm guarantee this? The reasoning (termination + backtracking attribution) is not explicit. Reader must trust the invariant or reconstruct the proof from code.

Test uses `make_proc()`, `_lists_one_step()`, `ByteFallbackTokenizer()` helpers not defined in the chapter. Intent is clear, but reader cannot independently run or verify the test without seeing mock implementations.
Text mentions slicing a FlatLogprobs returns '0-indexed shifted new FlatLogprobs' but the implementation is not shown. How exactly are start_indices and end_indices recomputed? Left to inference.

Decision to append(None) for prompt first token is in §10.2, but consequence (creates zero-length start==end interval in FlatLogprobs) is mentioned in §10.6. A reader jumping to §10.6 first would not understand why an interval can be zero-length.

Code assumes tokenizer may do 'normalization' causing prefix mismatch, so it computes longest common prefix as fallback. This is pragmatic but suggests tokenizer behavior is complex and unpredictable. Reader unfamiliar with SentencePiece/BPE quirks may not expect this, and the brief comment doesn't explain when/why this happens.

diagram_suggestions.what: Side-by-side diagram showing how _get_sampled_context_ids extracts first token_id from each position, contrasting FlatLogprobs path (access token_ids[start_indices[i]]) vs list[dict] path (next(iter(entry))). Would clarify the 'first entry is sampled token' invariant before diving into byte-fallback logic.

Flowchart or step-by-step diagram of _correct_decoded_token with a concrete 4-byte example (e.g., emoji U+1F600 = F0 9F 98 80). Show: iteration 1 with context [F0] → ends U+FFFD → continue; iteration 2 [F0, 9F] → ends U+FFFD → continue; iteration 3 [F0, 9F, 98] → ends U+FFFD → continue; iteration 4 [F0, 9F, 98, 80] → ends with emoji → backtrack → all 3 fallback → clean_end=0 → return emoji. Would nail down the termination + pairing guarantee concretely.

Pseudocode or detailed diagram of FlatLogprobs.__getitem__ slice operation. Show: original FlatLogprobs has positions 0,1,2 with start/end indices; slicing [1:] produces new FlatLogprobs with adjusted indices (position 0 in new structure maps to old position 1, etc.). Clarifies how nested list slicing is equivalent to interval remapping.

notes: Chapter is well-structured and mostly followable: two swimlanes, clear dispatch logic, solid code examples. Main friction is in §10.5.3 (byte-fallback correction algorithm) where the backtracking logic requires code inspection and the pairing invariant is stated without proof. Test helpers are not defined. A few forward references to FlatLogprobs structure before it's fully explained. Adding a 4-byte emoji example diagram and showing FlatLogprobs.__getitem__ slice logic would significantly reduce confusion in the technical core (§10.5)."
